Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain.

FOXA1 is a transcriptional pioneer factor recurrently mutated in human prostate cancer (primary, mCRPC, NEPC; mutation rates vary by cohort). FOXA1 mutants alter chromatin accessibility and cistromes (changing pioneer activity, motif preference, and chromatin-opening kinetics) and modify FOXA1–androgen receptor (AR) interactions (mutations reported to either enhance AR transcriptional signatures or inhibit AR binding depending on residue and model). Independently, certain FOXA1 truncation and Forkhead mutants activate EMT and metastasis, potentially via loss of TLE3 interaction and WNT/β‑catenin activation. FOXA1 activity is also regulated post‑translationally (LSD1 demethylation of K270 is required for optimal chromatin binding), leading to altered AR signaling. Outcomes include stage‑specific effects on proliferation, EMT/metastasis, biomarker potential in liquid biopsy, and therapeutic implications (e.g., targeting LSD1 or EMT pathways).
References: Teng, M., Zhou, S., Cai, C., Lupien, M., & He, H. H. (2021). Pioneer of prostate cancer: past, present and the future of FOXA1. Protein Cell, 12(1), 29-38. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13238-020-00786-8
